fed_monetary: The Fed holds the federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75% for a second consecutive meeting, with the April 28–29 FOMC decision universally expected to deliver another hold. March FOMC minutes (voted 11-1) confirm policymakers remain in patient-watch mode, with the lone dissent favoring a 25bp cut; however, PPI at 6.03% vs. a 2.6% estimate and Core PCE at 2.97% materially complicate any near-term pivot. The Fed's balance sheet stands at $6.71T (WALCL), reserves at $3.13T remain ample, and with RRP near zero, residual liquidity drain is unlikely to provide additional tightening impulse in the near term.
labor_data: March NFP printed +178K against a consensus of +100K, the strongest beat in the current cycle, while the unemployment rate held steady at 4.3%—exactly in line with estimates. Weekly initial claims of 207K (vs. 210K est.) confirm no deterioration in the flow of layoffs, and the insured unemployment rate remains at 1.2%. The labor market's resilience is a key impediment to Fed easing, though a St. Louis Fed model suggests payroll growth as low as 15K could stabilize the unemployment rate, meaning the current pace provides meaningful buffer before any softening forces policy reconsideration.
fiscal_supply: The March 2026 federal deficit printed $249B after timing adjustments—approximately 2% wider than March 2025—reflecting persistent structural imbalances even as the economy expands. The TGA stands at $751.4B, providing Treasury with a substantial cash cushion, but the quarterly refunding schedule signals continued heavy note and bond issuance through H1 2026. With the 10Y yield at 4.32% and the composite liquidity score at the 39th percentile, incremental Treasury supply faces an increasingly price-sensitive buyer base, and any auction tail risk could exert renewed upward pressure on term premia.
corporate: Q1 2026 S&P 500 earnings season arrives with an estimated net profit margin of 13.2%—above the five-year average of 12.2%—but tariff pass-through risks loom as the largest known unknown; Trump tariffs represent the biggest US tax increase as a share of GDP since 1993, and analysts are flagging meaningful margin compression risk in goods-exposed sectors. WTI surged 6.2% to $87.72 on the US Navy's seizure of an Iranian-flagged vessel and Strait of Hormuz escalation, a direct input-cost shock that will pressure corporate margins in transportation, chemicals, and consumer staples in coming quarters. HY OAS at 283bp and IG OAS at 101bp remain historically tight, but a rising VIX (18.99, +8.7%) and oil shock represent nascent headwinds to risk appetite.
next_catalyst: The April 28–29 FOMC meeting is the single most important near-term event; the post-meeting statement and Powell press conference will be closely parsed for any shift in the Committee's reaction function given the PPI overshoot, the geopolitical oil shock, and the ongoing tariff-inflation transmission debate.
sources: Fed Expected to Hold Rates at April 28–29 Meeting Amid Middle East Risks — J.P. Morgan Global Research (April 17, 2026)
